data.title: Former BOJ Board Member Eyes Possibility of Rate Hike in May — Interview
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--The Bank of Japan could raise interest rates again in May--earlier than most expect--if it opts to move before the political situation at home worsens and President Trump's tariffs cause a shock, says former BOJ board member Makoto Sakurai.

"June would be the most reasonable time to raise rates. However, it is unclear what would happen to [Prime Minister Shigeru] Ishiba's administration by then, and it may be difficult to move in July right after the upper house election," Sakurai told The Wall Street Journal.

Delivering a rate hike at its April 30-May 1 meeting would give the central bank lots of time to think about further tightening if needed, he added. He expects the BOJ to raise rates twice during the year ending in March 2026, bringing the policy rate to 1%.

A move in May would surprise many economists and investors, who pencil in the timing of the next increase around the summer instead, meaning that the bank is expected to hold steady at its meeting in March. But Sakurai, who was on the central bank's board under former governor Haruhiko Kuroda for five years, thinks the BOJ could choose to act sooner rather than later to avoid getting shackled by political instability.

Prime Minister Ishiba's cabinet has been suffering from weak approval ratings as a slush fund scandal engulfed the ruling party, ultimately costing it its majority in the lower house of parliament and casting doubt over the administration's ability to govern.

He now faces a critical test at the upcoming election due by late July. If the ruling coalition loses again, it will be dealt another blow that could further complicate policymaking. Political turbulence would likely make the BOJ wary about tightening policy and adding to potential risks to consumer sentiment and economic activity.

The Japanese central bank may also want to raise rates before potential tariffs under President Trump's administration hurt the Japanese economy, Sakurai said. Higher tariffs will likely weigh on Japanese exports, especially if the U.S. imposes 25% auto tariffs as Trump has said. In 2024, about 30% of Japan's shipments to the U.S. were motor vehicles.

And judging by the BOJ's decision to raise the policy rate to 0.5% in January without waiting for further clarity on Trump's policies, it may well favor acting pre-emptively again, Sakurai said. "It was a very interesting decision in terms of predicting the future," he added.

Though consensus is for a summertime rate hike, expectations in financial markets for an earlier move have been growing in recent weeks, given indicators signaling Japan's economic strength and hawkish messages by some BOJ policymakers.

Another key element will be the BOJ's first economic projections for the year ending in March 2028. If the board sees core consumer inflation and overall growth continuing to increase by around 2% and 1% respectively, that would justify a rate hike in May, Sakurai said. The BOJ will release its economic projections for that year alongside the rate decision.

The former board member expects the central bank to aim to eventually lift the policy rate to 1.5% in the year ending in March 2027. This would give the BOJ room to cut rates in case of an economic downturn, while keeping monetary conditions accommodative if inflation stays near the 2% target.

Once the policy rate climbs above 1%, that will likely cause some waves among Japanese consumers, who will begin to feel the pinch in areas like housing loans, Sakurai said.

With little complaints expected before that threshold, "The BOJ likely wants to reach 1% as soon as possible," he said.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Gabungan AQRS Unit, Partner Sue Turnpike Synergy for MYR501 Million
data.description:
Gabungan AQRS unit Gabungan Strategik and Syarikat Muhibah Perniagaan dan Pembinaan filed a civil suit on Feb. 14 against Turnpike Synergy and four others.

The dispute involves the SUKE Project, where Syarikat Muhibah Perniagaan dan Pembinaan awarded Gabungan Strategik won a subcontract in 2016 for bridge structure works.

The plaintiffs are seeking 501.3 million ringgit in damages, claiming the defendants maliciously administered the project to their detriment.

data.title: Siemens Raises $1.5 Billion From Siemens Healthineers Stake Sale
data.description:
By Helena Smolak

Siemens will raise around 1.45 billion euros ($1.51 billion) from the sale of its 2% stake in Siemens Healthineers.

The German technology company said Wednesday evening that it sold 26.5 million ordinary shares of the medical-technology maker at a price of 54.65 euros a share, representing a 2.9% discount to its closing price on Wednesday of 56.28 euros.

Its net proceeds from the sale, expected to close on Feb. 4, will fund general corporate purposes.

The shares were sold exclusively to qualified investors in a private placement through an accelerated bookbuilding process, it said.

Following the transactions, Siemens agreed to not sell shares for 90 days as part of a customary lock-up agreement. It currently holds a 73% stake in Siemens Healthineers.

The move comes after Siemens said in December that it would consider to reduce its majority stake in Siemens Healthineers, which it had listed in 2018, by around 5%.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: AlphaValue/Baader Europe Lifts BBVA Outlook on Strong Trading Gains
data.description:
AlphaValue/Baader Europe upgraded its near-term earnings outlook for Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria , citing stronger trading gains and operational improvements for the Spanish lender.

For 2025, the research firm now expects BBVA's EPS to reach 1.77 euros, up from its previous forecast of 1.64 euros. The 2026 estimate was also lifted to 1.89 euros from 1.76 euros. "In 2025, the upgrade is attributable to increased trading gains. In 2026, it is attributable to a combination of increased trading gains, better cost control, and lower loan impairments," the analyst wrote.

AlphaValue/Baader Europe also revised its net asset value estimate for BBVA to 18.3 euros per share from 15.6 euros, projecting a return on equity of 17.3% within two years.

The research firm lifted its rating on the stock to buy from add, with a target price of 16.5 euros.

(07:34 GMT) Anglo American Posts $3 Billion Loss After Large De Beers Impairment

By Adam Whittaker

Anglo American posted a worse loss than expected as the miner booked $3.8 billion in net impairments, including $2.9 billion at its De Beers diamond business.

The London-listed company reported a net loss of $3.1 billion for 2024 compared with the $116.9 million loss analysts had expected, according to a Visible Alpha compiled consensus.

The diversified miner reported underlying earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization--its preferred profit metric--of $8.46 billion compared with the $8.76 billion analysts had expected and the $9.96 billion it booked in 2022.

Anglo said its work to separate De Beers is well under way but wrote down the value of the diamond business by $2.9 billion, citing prevailing marking conditions.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: MSFT: Microsoft Stock Jumps 1% on Quantum Chip Powered by New State of Matter
data.description:
Key points:
  • Microsoft shares rise just 1%
  • Tech titan teases new state of matter
  • Quantum computing making progress?

Are you quantum-ready? Following Google’s quantum leap, Microsoft said it has invented a new state of matter to power its quantum chip.

🤖 Microsoft Shares Steady After Big Blog Drop

  • Microsoft stock powered (only?) 1% on Wednesday as investors didn’t show much of a reaction to some potentially game-changing news. The tech giant’s vice president of quantum computing Chetan Nayak took to the company’s blog section to publish a piece on a product he billed as a “breakthrough.”
  • It’s a new quantum chip that’s powered by (don’t mess it up now) topological superconductivity, “a new state of matter that previously existed only in theory.”

👋 Meet Majorana 1

  • The new quantum hardware — Majorana 1 — is a quantum processor similar to transistors in traditional computers’ semiconductor chips with the difference that it’s beefed up to hold 1 million topological qubits. What’s that? We’ll let the PMK explain it.
  • “The basic structure of a topological qubit places a suitable semiconductor nanowire in close proximity to a superconductor. Due to the proximity, the semiconductor nanowire also becomes superconducting and under the right conditions (including an appropriate magnetic field along the wire and voltages applied to the device) the semiconductor nanowire enters the topological phase.”

👉 First Parallel Universes, Now a New State of Matter?

  • “It’s perhaps not surprising that quantum computation would require us to engineer a new state of matter specifically designed to enable it,” Nayak wrote.
  • Microsoft believes that this new invention will open the door to manufacturing commercially usable and viable quantum computing within five years. The announcement comes a couple of months after Google unveiled a similar thing with its parallel universes teaser.

data.title: What are the main events for today?
data.description:
The European session is pretty empty on the data front with just a couple of low tier releases. The only highlight today is the US Jobless Claims data in the American session.

13:30 GMT/08:30 ET - US Jobless Claims

The US Jobless Claims continue to be one of the most important releases to follow every week as it’s a timelier indicator on the state of the labour market.

Initial Claims remain inside the 200K-260K range created since 2022, while Continuing Claims continue to hover around cycle highs although we’ve seen some easing recently.

This week Initial Claims are expected at 215K vs. 213K prior, while Continuing Claims are seen at 1871K vs. 1850K prior.

US Jobless Claims

Central bank speakers:

  • 09:00 GMT - ECB's Rehn (dove - voter)
  • 13:00 GMT/08:00 ET - ECB's Makhlouf (neutral - voter)
  • 16:00 GMT/11:00 ET - ECB's Nagel (hawk - voter)
  • 14:35 GMT/09:35 ET - Fed's Goolsbee (neutral - non voter)
  • 17:05 GMT/12:05 ET - Fed's Musalem (neutral - voter)
  • 19:30 GMT/14:30 ET - Fed's Barr (dove - voter)
  • 22:00 GMT/17:00 ET - Fed's Kugler (dove - voter)

data.title: Anglo American Swings to FY24 Loss, Revenue Down
data.description:
Anglo American (, AGL.JO) said Thursday it swung to a loss in 2024, while revenue declined year over year.

Loss attributable to equity shareholders for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was $3.07 billion, compared with a profit of $283 million earlier. Loss per share was $2.53, compared with an EPS of $0.23.

The mining giant's revenue was $27.29 billion, compared with $30.65 billion a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a final a dividend of $0.22 per share for the period payable on May 7 to shareholders on record March 14. It paid $0.41 per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to $0.64 per share, down from $0.96 in the previous year.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0728 GMT - The FTSE 100 is expected to open 14 points higher, or 0.2%, according to IG. The index closed 54 points lower, or 0.6%, to 8712 points Wednesday. Markets are dominated by headlines around talks for a Ukraine peace deal and potential spending from European countries, Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar says in a note. It is "clear that Europe will need to increase its defense spending," he says. This should be positive for risky assets with sectors including defense, machinery and financials likely to benefit. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve's meeting minutes Wednesday didn't provide much new information about future policy with the central bank indicating it is in no rush to cut interest rates further. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0713 GMT - Expectations for higher debt supply due to defense spending keep eurozone government bonds under pressure, says Danske Bank Research's August Hyldgaard in a note. "Expectations for higher debt issuance and a string of better-than-expected data, and less geopolitical turmoil may all be attributable to this," the assistant analyst says. Eurozone bonds were sold Wednesday, pushing 10-year bond yields higher by up to 9 bps, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.55% on Wednesday, the highest level since the end of January, according to Tradeweb data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - Malaysia's central bank may cut its policy rate by 25 bps in May, Barclays economist Brian Tan says, though the timing remains uncertain and dependant on U.S. tariff announcements. BNM reacted quickly to cut during the 2019 Trump tariff era and could do so again if it assesses that U.S. tariffs will hit Malaysia hard, he notes. Malaysia's 4Q GDP print showed a sequential contraction and the backdrop is likely to darken once more tariffs materialize, he says. Consensus sees concerns about the inflationary impact of fuel subsidy reforms as a constraint to rate cuts, but Barclays argues that BNM prioritizes broader economic conditions over inflation targeting. As growth cools and risks rise, it is unlikely to be so confident on the Malaysian economy that it will look past tariffs, Tan says. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0651 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 0.7% to close at 2654.06, snapping a seven-session winning streak. Foreign and institutional investors were net sellers, as some booked profit. Shipbuilding stocks led the retreat on profit taking after solid recent gains. Shipbuilder HD Hyundai Heavy Industries slumped 12% as its intermediate holding company HD Korea Shipbuilding & Offshore Engineering also dropped 9.0%. Both stocks lost ground after two straight days of gains. Chip maker SK Hynix fell 3.0%. USD/KRW settled almost flat at 1,437.90 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was down 0.9 basis point at 2.864%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0646 GMT - ​Long-dated government bond yields in developed markets have moved in tandem with those of U.S. Treasurys in recent months, but they will likely diverge in coming months, Capital Economics' Diana Iovanel says in a note. One explanation for the similarity in yield moves is that government bonds reflect common global factors which may affect bonds both in the U.S. and in other developed markets, the senior market economist says. Capital Economics expects the U.S. tariff effect on inflation in other developed markets to be generally limited. "We expect some of the rise in bond yields over recent months to unwind and domestic monetary policy to be the main driver of developed market yields over the rest of 2025." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0620 GMT - Communication by Chinese policymakers suggests they are likely to react not "pre-act" to potential tariff hikes, Goldman Sachs economists say. This implies a relatively low chance that 2025 budget numbers at the "Two Sessions" meeting in March will significantly beat expectations, they write. But if growth headwinds strengthen sharply, the door is open to additional stimulus beyond the budget, they say. GS expects China to keep real GDP growth target unchanged at around 5% for 2025, lower the consumer inflation target to around 2% from around 3%, and raise the official fiscal deficit target to 4.0% of GDP from 3.0% in 2024. Quotas for central and local government special bonds could be raised too. With consumption a top priority, China may also unveil details on consumption stimulus, GS says. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0532 GMT - The Bank of Thailand may consider different easing measure than rate cut next week, HSBC Global Research's Aris Dacanay says in a research report. This is because the BOT, in cooperation with fiscal authorities, is poised to implement a debt relief program for households and small- and medium-sized enterprises, the Asean economist says. The program's key feature is that domestic banks will partially absorb costs of the program via net interest margins, the economist says. A rate cut just when the program starts could be a blow to banks' net interest margins. However, fiscal policy is losing traction and some monetary-policy support may be needed, HSBC says and expects the BOT to cut rates by 25bps each in 2Q and 4Q. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0504 GMT - Bank Indonesia could cut rates twice by 25 bps each in 2Q and 3Q, bringing the policy rate to 5.25% where it could remain for the rest of 2025, UOB economists Enrico Tanuwidjaja and Vincentius Ming Shen say in a note. Based on BI's recent signals, it remains optimistic about Indonesia's economic resilience amid steady consumption and investment demand, despite internal and external headwinds, they note. They think BI's stance could indicate the potential for further cuts, but the timing will depend on economic conditions. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0620 GMT - Communication by Chinese policymakers suggests they are likely to react not "pre-act" to potential tariff hikes, Goldman Sachs economists say. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0532 GMT - The Bank of Thailand may consider different easing measure than rate cut next week, HSBC Global Research's Aris Dacanay says in a research report. This is because the BOT, in cooperation with fiscal authorities, is poised to implement a debt relief program for households and small- and medium-sized enterprises, the Asean economist says. However, fiscal policy is losing traction and some monetary-policy support may be needed, HSBC says and expects the BOT to cut rates by 25bps each in 2Q and 4Q. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0504 GMT - Bank Indonesia could cut rates twice by 25 bps each in 2Q and 3Q, bringing the policy rate to 5.25% where it could remain for the rest of 2025, UOB economists Enrico Tanuwidjaja and Vincentius Ming Shen say in a note. Based on BI's recent signals, it remains optimistic about Indonesia's economic resilience amid steady consumption and investment demand, despite internal and external headwinds, they note. They think BI's stance could indicate the potential for further cuts, but the timing will depend on economic conditions. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0345 GMT - China will likely roll out more measures for the private sector after President Xi Jinping's meeting with business leaders earlier this week, HSBC economists say in a note. They describe the meeting as timely, noting that homegrown DeepSeek's recent AI breakthrough and the record box-office performance of Chinese animated film "Ne Zha 2" have reinvigorated optimism around the private sector's development. China's draft private sector promotion law is up for its second review at the National People's Congress meeting in March, the economists note. It may lead to "a swift implementation and offer more reassurance of Beijing's commitment to supporting the private sector," they say. Meanwhile, Shanghai's announcement of consumption vouchers may be a precursor to an expansion of consumption promotion policies nationwide, they say. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0328 GMT - The Singapore dollar slightly strengthens against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session amid lower Treasury yields, which typically decreases the appeal of U.S. fixed-income assets and demand for the greenback. Traders are likely assessing various risks as well, analysts say. U.S. President Trump's tariff threats still loom, but it's unclear whether the tariffs will actually be enacted or be used as negotiating tools, note Maybank analysts in a FX Research & Strategy report. In addition, a peace deal for Ukraine remains uncertain, particularly with Trump calling Ukrainian President Zelensky a "dictator without elections," the analysts note. USD/SGD is 0.1% lower at 1.3409. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0234 GMT - The Bank of Korea is expected to resume its rate cut next week to support growth. All but one in a WSJ survey of 27 economists expect the BOK to cut its base rate by 25 bps to 2.75% at its policy meeting on Feb. 25. "Growth remains weak," HSBC economist Jin Choi says, citing downside risks to growth due to rising U.S. tariffs. The volatile won-dollar trade last month let the BOK stand pat following back-to-back rate cuts in October and November. But the recently easing forex volatility may allow for another cut, Choi adds. The BOK is also expected to lower its 2025 growth forecast. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0217 GMT - The euro-yen pair is forming a major bearish reversal pattern, based on the daily chart, Oanda's Kelvin Wong says in an email. Its swing highs of Nov. 16, 2023, July 11, 2024, and Oct. 31, 2024, have formed an impending major bearish reversal "head and shoulders" pattern, the senior market analyst says. This follows the currency pair's break below the support on a former long-term ascending channel in August. The 155.45 level marks the support on the neckline of the head and shoulders pattern, Wong notes. A daily close below that level could open the scope to trigger a major multimonth downtrend that may expose the next medium-term support levels of 151.00 and 145.60, the analyst adds. EUR/JPY is 0.6% lower at 156.98. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: German Producer Prices Rise Less than Estimated
data.description:
Producer prices in Germany increased by 0.5% year-on-year in January 2025, easing from December's 1-1/2-year high of 0.8% while marking the third straight month of rise.

The latest result was below market consensus for a 1.3% rise, attributed to a slight drop in prices of intermediate goods (-0.1%).

Meanwhile, energy prices fell by 1.0%, mainly due to lower costs of natural gas (-1.9%), electricity (-1.8%), and district heating (-1.5%) despite a 0.7% rise in mineral oil products.

Meanwhile, higher prices were seen for non-durable consumer goods (3.0%) and durable consumer goods (1.1% vs 0.4%).

Notably, capital goods rose further (1.9%), especially machinery (1.9%) and motor vehicles, trailers, and semi-trailers (1.4%).

Excluding energy, producer prices rose by 1.2%.

On a monthly basis, producer prices edged down by 0.1%, keeping the same pace as in December while missing consensus of a 0.6% gain.

In 2024, factory-gate prices shrank by 1.8%, compared to a 0.2% rise in 2023.

data.title: Mercedes-Benz CFO: No Increase in Tariffs From Current Levels Are Included in Guidance
Turkey Consumer Confidence Highest Since 2023
data.description:
The consumer confidence index in Turkey rose to 82.1 in February 2025, marking its highest level since June 2023, up from 81 in January.

All subindexes showed improvement, including households' financial situation expectations for the next 12 months (83.1 vs. 80.5), general economic situation expectations for the same period (79.3 vs. 79.1), and spending propensity over the year ahead (100.3 vs. 94).

data.title: China Tower Gets Green Light for Share Consolidation
data.description:
China Tower has received approval from the listing committee of the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong to list and deal in its proposed consolidated and reduced H shares, according to a bourse filing on Thursday.

Back in November 2024, the telecommunications infrastructure firm announced its plan to consolidate its shares.

The consolidation will see every 10 existing shares with a par value of 1 yuan each combined into one new share, also with a par value of 1.00 yuan. This move will reduce the company's total issued share capital to 17.6 billion yuan from 176.0 billion yuan.

The consolidation and reduction will take effect on Thursday, and trading in such shares is expected to start on the same day.

(07:07 GMT) *Lloyds Banking Sees 2025 RoTE Around 13.5%
(07:07 GMT) *Analysts See Lloyds Banking 2025 RoTE 9.8%
(07:07 GMT) *Lloyds Banking Backs 2026 View
(07:08 GMT) *Lloyds Banking To Implement Up To GBP1.7B Share Buyback
(07:08 GMT) *Lloyds Banking Declares Dividend of 2.11p
(07:11 GMT) *Lloyds Banking Took GBP700M Provision in 4Q For Motor Finance
(07:30 GMT) Lloyds Banking Group Books Extra Provision For Motor Finance Probe, Denting Profit

By Elena Vardon

Lloyds Banking Group took a further provision in the fourth quarter for the potential hit of a possible redress linked to its motor finance business, leading its pretax profit for the period to miss expectations.

The British lender on Thursday said it set 700 million pounds ($881.1 million) aside to cover the potential impact of historical commission arrangements on car loans. It had already booked a 450 million pound provision at its third-quarter results.

The group has been grappling with the uncertainty in the motor-finance sector amid a landmark case and regulatory review. Lloyds is the largest provider of motor finance in the U.K. and is exposed to these issues through its Black Horse brand.

For the three months ended Dec. 31, Lloyds posted pretax profit of 824 million pounds compared with 1.775 billion pounds for the same period a year earlier and a 1.25 billion pound estimate taken from a company-compiled consensus.

The bank--which is the country's largest mortgage provider--reported an on-year 1% rise in net income to 4.38 billion pounds, slightly beating consensus' 4.34 billion pound estimate. Net interest income made up 3.28 billion pounds of the total, against expectations of 3.25 billion pounds.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: Investing in Private Markets Isn't for the Timid. An Industry Insider Explains Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Reshma Kapadia

Andrea Auerbach launched her career in private-market investing in the early 1990s, when the business was largely synonymous with leveraged buyouts. Today it encompasses venture capital, private equity, private credit, and much more. Interest in so-called alternative assets likewise has exploded: Assets under management are on track to hit $15 trillion this year, up from $10 trillion in 2021.

Auerbach, global head of private investments at Cambridge Associates, helps clients such as small charitable organizations, hospitals, universities, and sovereign-wealth funds source, underwrite, and ultimately invest about $12 billion in private markets. She has had a ringside seat to the industry's expansion, including its more recent struggles as interest rates have risen.

Many managers of private assets are now looking to grow by tapping retail investors, including through 401(k) assets. Barron's spoke with Auerbach in early February about the appeal of private markets, the outlook for fund-raising and deal activity, and the implications of the "democratization" of alternatives.

An edited version of the discussion follows.

Barron's : Why are private-market investments attracting so much attention — and dollars?

Andrea Auerbach: It comes down to the returns. On a 15-year basis, as of Sept. 30, private markets returned 15% net of fees, versus 10% for the MSCI All-Country World Index.

Public equity markets have become highly concentrated in technology stocks. We have seen this before, with mining and railroads once dominating the public markets. The growth of passive investing also complicates things: If you are investing passively in an index, how are you going to outperform? If investors want to outperform the market, they need to invest in other strategies that differ from the public markets.

But there are certain things private-market investors have to accept. Private investments are illiquid, your money may be tied up for a while, and you may not be able to access information about your investments.

Private-equity funds had a couple of years of muted returns and fund-raising difficulties as interest rates rose. What happened?

The amount of capital invested in 2021 was almost twice the long-term annual average when interest rates were held near zero. Markets were risk-on. Equity valuations rose; the meme stock phenomenon was born. People were investing as though the party wasn't going to end. The general partners [in private-equity funds] took on leverage assuming a certain interest rate, and paid a certain price for assets assuming that rates would stay low.

Then inflation appeared and interest rates rose, throwing a spanner in the works. If a fund wants to sell an asset for a gain, it needs to hold it for longer and do more to enhance its value. Exits from private-equity investments [through initial public offerings, mergers, or other actions that produce proceeds] came to a halt as the Federal Reserve raised rates in March 2022.

PE firms use leverage to varying degrees to acquire companies, and that leverage became more expensive as rates rose. Typically, [PE firms] raise a fund and start investing that capital, and then show some exits and positive [return] momentum so they can come back to investors and raise another fund. But when rates rose, they couldn't sell anything. The companies they had invested in were busy combating inflation and higher interest-rate costs, and weren't progressing well. Everything slowed down. Distributions [to investors], fund-raising, and transactions have all been down.

What is the outlook for this year?

We expect more exits this year. Private-equity funds have a time limit; they can't hold the assets forever. The legal term limit is 10 years plus two one-year extensions, and then we have to talk. The investment period is typically five years — during that time, a manager can charge a management fee for committed capital. After that investment period expires, the management fee is only paid on invested capital, so it drops if the money hasn't been put to work.

Think about the 2021 vintage year [funds created that year]. They are in year five. If the general partners want to raise their next funds, they need to distribute some cash to current investors. These investors are going to ask: What have you done for us? Show us the return. That is a motivating factor for exits.

Also, the businesses in which these funds invested have become more battle-tested. They have healed their supply chains and persevered through an inflationary period, potentially making them more attractive deal candidates.

Lastly, there is a lot of capital still sitting in larger funds that needs to be put to work. That will mean more activity, with smaller companies owned by private equity sold to larger companies. And we will see more private-equity funds taking public companies private.

Are you expecting a boom in mergers and acquisitions?

We expect to see a boost in M&A activity this year relative to last year, with the software and business-services sectors leading the way. More M&A activity means more companies are sold, and that means more distributions to investors.

Institutional investors tend to be patient with payouts, but how is the dearth of distributions impacting the private-equity industry? There is a heightened sense of urgency on the general-partner side to demonstrate that private-equity firms are doing what they were hired to do. The business model of private equity is predicated on always raising another fund. You assume you can raise some money and plan on generating management fees and carried interest [a portion of a fund's profits paid to the fund manager]. If that possibility goes away, your organization is at risk.

How does your team evaluate these situations?

We want to understand the operating performance of a fund's current holdings. We also want to understand the fund manager's exit views, including whether the fund is trying to sell these investments.

Given the industry's current difficulties, some investors have turned to the secondary market to sell their stakes in PE funds. That market has now grown to $152 billion, according to Lazard. How do you view this trend?

Some of our clients don't need cash immediately, so they are patient. But some — like pension managers facing a wave of pensioner retirements, or hospitals or families that want to be more philanthropic — may need the money sooner. The secondary market offers a way for institutional investors to get liquidity by putting their stakes in PE funds up for sale. Last year saw the largest number of first-timers bringing secondaries to sell.

Private credit, or nonbank lending, has become a $2 trillion business. What is the outlook, given the interest-rate backdrop?

Some of the families I work with are investing in private credit instead of what used to be called the hedge fund allocation as returns for private credit look more interesting now that rates have risen. If you can make a competitive return through senior lending, investing in the senior-most part of the capital structure, that is likely a better adjusted return than an equity strategy in a hedge fund.

There is a lot of dry powder in private credit. Spreads have tightened [between private credit and Treasury yields] given the amount of capital available, and the amount of leverage, which has gone up. Private credit continues to deliver good returns. Investors are also diversifying within the private-credit bucket. They are investing not just in senior lending but also in specialty finance and other forms of credit.

There is a push on Wall Street to democratize private-market investing and offer individual investors more access. Do you expect this effort to be successful?

The industry is actively courting retail investors, and there has been a significant increase in the number of solutions that firms are bringing to market for individuals. I expect the push to be successful.

The nature of vehicles available to individuals is a bit different from the traditional structure for institutional investors, who commit capital to a fund that will call it when needed. In the case of a semiliquid fund aimed at the retail market, an investor puts up all the money upfront, and the fund then invests it quickly in things that can deliver private-equity-like returns. Some of the money goes into private markets, but some has to be managed for liquidity.

Should retail investors expect more-muted returns than institutional investors?

Managers successful in bringing retail investors onto their platforms are now responsible for investing substantial capital, which can make it harder to earn competitive returns.

Some of the retail solutions in the market are buying secondaries because they have to get that money invested quickly. That creates pricing pressure in the secondary market, such that you probably want to sell secondaries and be careful if you want to buy.

But private markets still offer investors the ability to diversify and add a different source of exposure that they hadn't previously been able to access.

What does the potential avalanche of retail money coming into private markets mean for institutional investors?

It could negatively impact returns. Thinking through any necessary adjustments to our investment approach is top of mind. One thing we talk about with our institutional clients is, how do you invest successfully in a way that isn't in front of a steamroller [created by retail-oriented strategies]. We are advising clients to stay clear of those managers and platforms [that target retail investors]. You want to be where the crowd isn't.

What industry sectors are getting a lot of attention in private markets? In private equity, enterprise software has become the No. 1 investment sector. More broadly, technology, industrial/multisector, and healthcare have been the three big investment areas for a while. In 2023-24, software went through a recession; year-over-year revenue growth flattened. Industrials, too, hit an air pocket due to issues with supply chains, labor, and raw material inputs, and over-inventorying at customers. We are watching these sectors improve, and expect this year to be productive.

Thanks, Andrea.

Write to Reshma Kapadia at reshma.kapadia@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Swedish Capacity Utilization Rises in Q4
data.description:
Industries in Sweden operated at 87.7% capacity in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 0.7 percentage points from a downwardly revised 87% in the previous quarter.

Still, the reading remained below the 100% threshold, indicating underutilization.

The largest decline in capacity usage was attributed to insufficient demand, which fell from 55.8% to 54.2% quarter-over-quarter.

On an annual basis, capacity utilization declined by 0.9 percentage points, reaching 87.4%.

data.title: Silver Rises Amid Trade and Geopolitical Concerns
data.description:
Silver prices climbed toward $33 per ounce on Thursday, recovering losses from the previous session as trade and geopolitical uncertainties drove demand for safe-haven metals.

Earlier this week, US President Donald Trump announced plans to impose 25% tariffs on imports of automobiles, semiconductors, and pharmaceuticals, reigniting fears of a broader global trade war.

Market anxiety also deepened following Trump’s remarks calling Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy a dictator amid ongoing peace talks with Russia to end the war in Ukraine.

Meanwhile, US Federal Reserve officials noted in January that they would prefer to see more progress on inflation before cutting interest rates further, expressing concerns over the potential impact of Trump’s tariffs.

data.title: Danish Q4 GDP Growth Strongest in Nearly 2 Years
data.description:
Denmark’s economy advanced by 1.6% quarter-on-quarter in the fourth quarter of 2024, up from an upwardly revised 1.3% growth in the previous period, a preliminary estimate showed.

This marked the third consecutive quarter of expansion and the fastest growth since Q1 2023, driven mainly by the pharmaceutical industry.

Net external demand also contributed positively to the GDP, as exports rose (4.1% vs 0.6% in Q3) more than imports (0.7% vs -0.3%).

Additionally, government spending (1.6% vs. 0.8%) and household consumption (0.6% vs. 0.2%) increased, with notable growth in vehicle purchases (3% vs. 1.8%).

However, fixed investments fell sharply (-0.8% vs. 2.7%), weighed down by declines in housing (-2.1% vs. 5%), other construction and facilities (-0.5% vs. 2.6%), and machinery and transport equipment (-2.8% vs. 3.4%).

On an annual basis, GDP expanded by 4.1% in Q4, following an upwardly revised 3.8% in the preceding quarter.

data.title: LYC Healthcare Mulls Private Placement of Shares
data.description:
LYC Healthcare proposed to undertake a private placement of up to 10% of the total number of issued shares to third-party investors.

The healthcare company will determine an issue price later, according to a Wednesday Malaysian bourse filing.

data.title: EPS Creative Health to Offload R&D Wing
data.description:
EPS Creative Health Technology Group agreed to sell the entire issued share capital of EPS Innovative Medicine (Hong Kong) to controlling shareholder EPS Holdings for 1 yen, a Wednesday filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

The firm will also sell to EPS Holdings 1.25 billion yen of certain loans owed by the target company and EPD Hong Kong to the company, EPS Healthcare, and EPS Medical Consultancy Services.

The disposal is subject to approval from the garment and healthcare products company's shareholders.

EPS Innovative Medicine (Hong Kong) engages mainly in the R&D of regenerative medicine and search for functional foods, the development of new drugs and medical devices, and the promotion of in-house researches.

Shares of the company were up over 2% in recent trade.

data.title: UAE's Alpha Data Sets Price Range for Abu Dhabi IPO
data.description:
Alpha Data set the offer price for its initial public offering in Abu Dhabi at between 1.45 Emirati dirhams and 1.50 dirhams per share, according to a Thursday release.

The offer price implies a market capitalization of between 1.45 billion dirhams and 1.50 billion dirhams. The IT services company intends to offer 400 million shares, or 40% of its capital.

The company will disclose the final offer price on Feb. 26 following a book building process. The subscription period started Thursday and will finish on Feb. 25.

The shares are expected to start trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange on March 11.

data.title: Basler Kantonalbank's FY24 Profit Up; Operating Income Drops
data.description:
Basler Kantonalbank said Thursday that its profit for full-year 2024 increased, while operating income fell year over year.

Profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 172.9 million francs, compared with 157.4 million francs a year ago.

The Swiss lender's operating income was 492.3 million francs, compared with 497.4 million francs a earlier.

Meanwhile, the company declared a dividend of 4.50 francs per share for the period. It paid 3.25 francs per share a year ago.

data.title: Buy Life Insurance Corporation of India; target of Rs 1040: ICICI Securities
data.description:
ICICI Securities research report on Life Insurance Corporation of India

LIC has performed well with 9MFY25 VNB growth of 9.1% and H1FY25 EV growth of 24% YoY. Strategic initiatives have tracked well consistently in terms of pushing its product mix towards non-participating (28% of individual APE in 9MFY25 vs 18% in FY24 and 9% in FY23) and revision in pricing/product strategy to maximise value for all stakeholders with changes like hike in surrender value. There is expansion in non-agency distribution channels (5.3% of individual NBP in 9MFY25 vs 3.9%/4% in FY23/24), focus on agency (total number of agents stands at 1.4mn as of Dec’24, 3.3% YoY growth) and improvement in digital initiatives (DIVE/Jeevan Samarth). We believe product mix-driven possible rise in VNB margin is achievable and underappreciated by the market.

Outlook

Our valuations are based on 0.7x (earlier 0.8x) FY27E EV of INR 9.4trn. We have reduced our multiple to adequately reflect the risk of EV sensitivity to market movement and a lower core RoEV profile (compared to peers) on a high base. We estimate 1%/8%/7% change in weighted APE (0.3% in 10MFY25), VNB margin of ~17-18% (16.8%/17.1% in FY24/9MFY25) and unwinding of ~8.5% (FY24:9%) for FY25/26E/27E. We expect core RoEV at ~10% for FY25E/26E/27E (vs 11.5% in FY24).

For all recommendations report, click here

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts/broking houses/rating agencies on moneycontrol.com are their own, and not that of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

Life Insurance Corporation of India - 20022025 - isc

data.title: Tether leads $10 million funding in cross-border payments firm MANSA
data.description:
Cross-border payment services firm MANSA has completed a $10 million financing round led by USDT stablecoin issuer Tether, fueling the company's global expansion.

According to a press release, Tether led MANSA’s $3 million pre-seed funding that was co-led by Polymorphic Capital, with other participants including Octerra Capital, Faculty Group, and Trive Digital. MANSA said it secured an additional $7 million in liquidity funding from other institutional investors. 

MANSA provides onchain liquidity solution using USDT stablecoin to help make cross-border payments faster and more accessible for firms.

“MANSA’s vision for addressing liquidity challenges in cross-border payments aligns with our mission to create a more efficient and inclusive financial system,” Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino said. “By leveraging USDT for real-time settlements and instant payouts, MANSA is solving critical pain points for payment companies operating in emerging markets.”

The company said it plans to utilize the funding to support its expansion into Latin America and Southeast Asia, where cross-border transactions face liquidity challenges. 

Since launching in August 2024, MANSA has primarily focused on building partnerships with payment companies in Africa, Asia and South America. It has seen around $31 million in transaction volume on its platform to date, according to data from Dune Analytics.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.title: Hays Posts Decline in Fiscal H1 Profit, Turnover
data.description:
Hays said Thursday that its attributable profit and turnover for the fiscal first half declined year over year.

Profit attributable to equity holders of its parent company for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 3 million pounds sterling, compared with 12.3 million pounds a year ago. EPS moved to 0.0019 pound from 0.0077 pound.

The British recruitment company's turnover was 3.37 billion pounds, compared with 3.54 billion pounds a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board declared an unchanged interim dividend of 0.0095 pound per share for the period, payable April 9 to shareholders on record Feb. 28.

data.title: China Stocks End Flat as Trump Signals Potential Deal
data.description:
The Shanghai Composite closed nearly flat, while the Shenzhen Component gained 0.2% to 10,794 on Thursday, as Chinese stocks resisted a broader selloff across the Asia Pacific region.

US President Donald Trump suggested that a new trade deal with China was possible, signaling a positive outlook for relations with Beijing.

On the monetary policy front, the People's Bank of China kept benchmark lending rates unchanged to maintain financial stability.

The central bank held its one- and five-year loan prime rates at 3.1% and 3.6%, respectively, in line with market expectations.

Technology and AI-related stocks outperformed, with notable gains from Talkweb Information (+4.5%), DHC Software (+10%), Greatoo Intelligent (+3%), Shenzhen Everwin (+8.8%), and All Winner Technology (+6.8%).

data.title: Nordic Stocks Tipped to Open Slightly Higher — Market Talk
data.description: Nordic markets may open slightly higher with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.2% at around 2716. Yesterday's move by President Trump in calling President Zelenskyy a dictator and implying that Ukraine started the war, caused the euro to weaken, SEB analysts say in a note. "The prospects for continued U.S. support look increasingly bleak, and many European leaders are expressing concern about a deteriorating alliance with the U.S." The defense sector stood out Wednesday and rose among an otherwise sea of red of European equities, SEB adds. U.S. stock markets shook off geopolitics and cautious monetary policy minutes and rose marginally to record highs. Asian stock markets are declining this morning and European and U.S. futures prices point to a negative opening. OMXS30 closed at 2710.23, OMXN40 at 2630.08 and OBX at 1417.97. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Mercedes-Benz Expects Earnings Slump, Plans to Cut Costs — Update
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Mercedes-Benz expects earnings to fall significantly this year and outlined plans to boost competitiveness that includes a 10% cut to production costs over the next couple of years.

"To ensure the company's future competitiveness in an increasingly uncertain world, we are taking steps to make the company leaner, faster and stronger, while readying an intense product launch campaign for multiple new vehicles," said Chief Executive Ola Kaellenius.

European automakers have been scrambling to boost their competitiveness as they navigate stuttering demand for electric vehicles and intense competition in China, while a struggling Chinese economy has also seen Chinese buyers shy away from luxury spending.

At the same time, manufacturers face the prospect of an escalating global trade war, as the threat of tariffs on cars imported to the U.S. also hang over the industry.

The German luxury-car maker said Thursday that dozens of new or refreshed models will reach markets through 2027, which will help sales gain traction, while actions to make its global production footprint more efficient and more flexible and a focus on bringing down both material costs and fixed costs will also be implemented.

In addition, through unifying some aspects of its technology and design, it will be able to tailor products to specific markets like China, it said.

The company reported a 41% drop in earnings at its car unit last year on lower volumes, particularly in China, negative net pricing and an unfavorable model mix.

It expects to sell slightly fewer cars and vans this year compared with 2024 while its adjusted return on sales margin in the car unit is guided at 6%-8% compared with 8.1% in 2024.

Group revenue in 2025 will be slightly below the prior-year level and group earnings before interest and tax are expected to be significantly below the previous year's level. Group free cash flow from the industrial business is also seen significantly below the strong level of 2024, it said.

Mercedes lowered its dividend to 4.30 euros ($4.48) from 5.30 euros a share in 2023, and it has decided on share buybacks of up to 5 billion euros over a period of up to 24 months.

Fourth-quarter net profit fell to 2.48 billion euros from 3.12 billion euros a year prior, as revenue declined 3.8% to 38.45 billion euros.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected net profit to come in at 2.42 billion euros on revenue of 38.12 billion euros.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com
